name: Aether
description: Aether, in Greek mythology, is the personification of the upper sky, the pure, bright air breathed by the gods. He is the son of Erebus (Darkness) and Nyx (Night), and brother to Hemera (Day). He represents the higher, purer air above the clouds, where the gods reside, as opposed to the air breathed by mortals, which was represented by Aer. Aether is often depicted as the essence of the heavens, the radiant light that fills the upper atmosphere. He is considered the 'upper sky' or 'heavenly air,' distinct from the lower atmosphere, Aer. Aether's presence is often associated with divine radiance and purity. He is the very breath of the gods, the substance that connects them to the cosmos. While not featuring prominently in many myths, his symbolic importance lies in his representation of the divine realm and the ethereal nature of the gods' existence. Some accounts also consider him to be the father of Gaia (Earth) or Uranus (Sky), further solidifying his role as a primal force in creation. His power is subtle but pervasive, representing the very medium through which the gods communicate and exist. He represents the clarity of the heavens, the divine light, and the pure essence of the celestial realm. Aether is often invoked in poetic descriptions of the heavens and is a vital element in understanding the Greek concept of the cosmos.
subtitle: Personification of the Upper Sky

keyMyths.title: Origin of Aether
keyMyths.description: Aether is born from Erebus (Darkness) and Nyx (Night), representing the emergence of light from darkness.

keyMyths.title: Aether as the Breath of Gods
keyMyths.description: Aether is the pure air breathed by the gods, distinguishing their realm from the mortal world below.

keyMyths.title: Aether's Role in Creation
keyMyths.description: Some traditions suggest Aether as a father of Gaia (Earth) or Uranus (Sky), highlighting his primal role.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: Provides the genealogical origins of Aether.
